full_text: Topic 2 — 2.5 Spiral Model 2.5 Spiral Model ← Topic 2 The Spiral Model is a risk-driven SDLC model proposed by Barry Boehm in 1986. Unlike Classical Waterfall, which assumes risks can be ignored until late testing, the Spiral model makes risk analysis and risk reduction the organising principle of the project. Each iteration of the project is represented as one loop (cycle) of a spiral that grows outward: the team plans objectives, analyses risks, builds and tests product elements, evaluates with the customer, and then decides whether to proceed to the next, larger cycle. The Spiral model combines ideas from other approaches: structured phases like Waterfall, prototypes to resolve uncertainty (as in §2.4), and iterative delivery of increasing completeness. It is widely cited in exams as the model best suited to large, complex, high-risk software projects. Spiral Model — each revolution of the spiral is one iteration; cost and scope grow outward (GeeksforGeeks). Four quadrants (sectors) of each spiral cycle — memorise order and purpose for diagrams in exams. Core idea and philosophy Boehm observed that many failures come from unresolved risks — wrong requirements, new technology that does not scale, performance shortfalls, or staff turnover. The Spiral model forces the team to ask at every cycle: What can go wrong? How serious is it? What will we do now to reduce it? Only after risk-handling activities does the team commit to major development cost for that cycle. Each loop produces a more complete product (requirements slice, design, code, or operational release). Early loops may produce only documents and prototypes. Later loops deliver production-quality software. The customer is involved at the end of every cycle in evaluation, not only at project start and finish. Four quadrants of each spiral cycle Every revolution of the spiral is divided into four sectors (quadrants). In long answers, describe each quadrant with activities and outputs. Order matters for exams. Quadrant 1 — Determine objectives, alternatives, and constraints Planning for the current cycle. The team defines what this iteration must achieve, what resources are available, and what boundaries apply (budget, schedule, standards, technology choices). Activities. The team sets iteration goals, compares alternative solutions (for example database A versus B, or in-house hosting versus cloud), and records constraints from stakeholders and regulation. Outputs. This quadrant produces an iteration plan, an updated project schedule, and a clear definition of success for the current loop. Exam note. In an exam answer, state that this quadrant answers the question where are we going in this cycle? Quadrant 2 — Identify and resolve risks The distinctive quadrant of the Spiral model. Risks are identified, prioritised, and mitigated before heavy engineering. Mitigation often uses prototypes, simulations, benchmarks, or feasibility studies — linking directly to Software Prototyping (§2.4). Types of risk. The team considers technical risks (such as performance or security), schedule risks (such as dependency delays), cost risks (such as budget overrun), business risks (such as market change), and staffing risks (such as losing a key person). Activities. The team runs risk workshops, performs probability and impact analysis, builds throwaway prototypes to test the user interface, runs technical spikes to test API throughput, and arranges legal reviews for compliance where needed. Outputs. Typical outputs include a risk register, a risk mitigation plan, and prototypes or proof-of-concept results that reduce uncertainty. Exam note. If a risk cannot be reduced to an acceptable level, the project may stop or pivot early, which saves cost compared with discovering failure only at the end of a Waterfall project. Quadrant 3 — Develop and test Engineering work for this cycle: whatever was planned once risks are understood. May include requirements specification for a subsystem, detailed design, coding, integration, and testing. Activities. The team produces the deliverables agreed in quadrant 1, carries out unit, integration, and system testing, and holds technical reviews and inspections. Outputs. Outputs include code, design documents, test reports, and a working increment of the system for this cycle. Exam note. This quadrant resembles Waterfall phases, but the work is scoped to one spiral cycle, not to the entire project at once. Quadrant 4 — Plan the next iteration Customer evaluation and decision-making. Stakeholders review progress against objectives, approve the current increment, and plan the next loop (or terminate the project). Activities. The team demonstrates results to the customer, collects feedback, updates the requirements backlog, and decides the budget and scope for the next spiral. Outputs. This quadrant produces an evaluation report, approval to continue (or a decision to stop), and a revised plan for the next cycle. Exam note. This quadrant is also called customer evaluation and should be linked to stakeholder management and acceptance criteria. Two dimensions of the Spiral model Boehm describes progress in two dimensions. Draw the spiral and label both in exam diagrams. Angular dimension (horizontal rotation) — represents progress through the SDLC phases (requirements, design, implementation, testing) as the project moves around the spiral. Each full loop advances the product through more of the lifecycle. Over many cycles, the project covers the same kinds of work as Waterfall, but in slices. Radial dimension (outward movement) — represents cumulative cost, effort, and scope. As the spiral grows outward, more money and people are committed; the system becomes larger and more complete. Outer loops are more expensive than inner loops. Memory aid: angular = which phase angle are we at?; radial = how big and costly is the project now? How one full spiral cycle works (exam scenario) Cycle goal: prove that a new payment gateway can handle peak load for an e-commerce platform. Q1 — Objectives: Define success as 10,000 transactions/minute with 99.9% success rate. Budget two weeks. Q2 — Risks: Unknown latency from third-party API. Mitigate with load-test prototype and failover design review. Q3 — Develop/test: Implement integration module and run automated load tests. Fix bottlenecks. Q4 — Evaluation: Show results to customer. Approve next cycle for full checkout UI integration, or cancel gateway if risks remain too high. Spiral Model vs other models AspectWaterfallPrototype ModelSpiral Model Main driverWaterfall is driven by completing sequential phases in order.The Prototype model is driven by user feedback on working prototypes.The Spiral model is driven by explicit risk reduction in every cycle. IterationClassical Waterfall has no iteration, or feedback only when errors are found.Iteration continues until requirements are clear enough to build the final system.The team runs planned cycles until the full product is complete. PrototypingPrototyping is optional and not central to Waterfall.Prototyping is the central activity of the model.Prototypes are used in the risk quadrant whenever uncertainty must be reduced. Project sizeWaterfall suits small or simple projects with stable scope.The Prototype model suits projects with unclear requirements.The Spiral model suits large, high-risk projects. Cost of failureIn Waterfall, the cost of failure is often discovered late in the project.Prototype models reduce the cost of wrong requirements through early evaluation.The Spiral model reduces the cost of major risks by addressing them early in each cycle. Advantages of the Spiral Model Explicit risk management — risks are documented and addressed every cycle; suitable for safety-critical, financial, aerospace, and large enterprise systems. Early elimination of bad options — prototypes and studies in quadrant 2 prevent building the wrong architecture for years. Flexible use of prototyping — only wh

When NOT to use (or use with care)
Summary table — evolutionary models at a glance
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.6 Evolutionary Development Models 2.6 Evolutionary Development Models ← Topic 2 Evolutionary development models treat software as something that grows and changes over time rather than being fully specified and built in a single pass. The system is developed through a sequence of versions (increments), where each version refines, extends, or corrects the previous one. Requirements, des
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.6 Evolutionary Development Models 2.6 Evolutionary Development Models ← Topic 2 Evolutionary development models treat software as something that grows and changes over time rather than being fully specified and built in a single pass. The system is developed through a sequence of versions (increments), where each version refines, extends, or corrects the previous one. Requirements, design, and implementation co-evolve as stakeholders learn from each release — in contrast to Waterfall, which tries to freeze requirements before construction. “Evolutionary” is a family of approaches, not one diagram with a single name. Several named SDLC models — evolutionary prototyping (§2.4), Spiral (§2.5), Incremental (§2.8), and Agile (§2.11) — are all evolutionary in spirit. Exams often ask for the general concept first, then examples. Evolution of SDLC methodologies — evolutionary approaches emphasise successive refinement and adaptation (GeeksforGeeks). Definition and core idea In evolutionary development, the team delivers an initial version quickly with the most important or best-understood functionality. Users and developers learn from that version; the next version adds features, improves quality, or fixes misunderstandings. The final product is reached by successive refinement, not by executing one frozen plan from day one. Key phrase for exams: “The system evolves from a partial, imperfect first version into a complete product through multiple iterations.” Key characteristics of evolutionary development Partial early delivery — users receive usable software (or a strong prototype) long before the full feature set exists; feedback drives later versions. Co-evolving requirements and design — the SRS is not fixed at the start; it is updated after each release based on experience. Adaptation over prediction — the process embraces change in business needs, technology, and understanding rather than treating change as failure of planning. Successive versions — version 1.0, 1.1, 2.0, or sprint releases each build on the previous codebase or design. User-centred learning — especially when UI or workflow is critical; same motivation as prototyping, but evolutionary models may deliver production code each cycle. Architecture must be managed — without refactoring and standards, repeated extension leads to unmaintainable “big ball of mud” systems. Evolutionary vs other development styles AspectWaterfall (classical)Evolutionary development RequirementsIn classical Waterfall, requirements are treated as fixed early in the project.In evolutionary development, requirements emerge and change across successive versions. Planning horizonWaterfall plans the whole project in detail at the start.Evolutionary development plans the next version in detail while keeping only a high-level roadmap for the whole effort. First user-visible resultWaterfall delivers a user-visible result late in the lifecycle.Evolutionary development delivers a core product or release early. Response to changeWaterfall tends to resist change because changes are costly after sign-off.Evolutionary development expects change and plans for it in each new version. Risk of wrong productWaterfall carries a high risk of building the wrong product if requirements were wrong from the start.Evolutionary development lowers that risk because mistakes can be corrected in later versions. Major examples of evolutionary models (know each for exams) 1. Evolutionary prototyping (Prototype Model — §2.4) The prototype becomes the product. Version 1 is a minimal prototype; later versions add modules and refactor. Same codebase grows until it is the delivered system. Best when requirements are unclear but something usable must appear quickly. Strength. This approach gives fast feedback and shows continuous visible progress to stakeholders. Weakness. Technical debt can grow quickly if code quality is ignored between versions. 2. Spiral model (§2.5) Each spiral cycle delivers a more complete evolutionary increment while emphasising risk reduction before engineering. Evolution happens around the spiral; risk analysis distinguishes Spiral from simple “build version 1, then version 2” without structure. 3. Incremental process model (§2.8) The system is split into increments (chunks of functionality). Each increment is often developed like a mini-Waterfall and delivered to the customer. Overall architecture is usually planned early; evolution is in features delivered, not in discovering the whole architecture from scratch. Contrast. The Incremental model uses planned partitions of functionality, whereas pure evolutionary prototyping may let architecture emerge over time. 4. Agile software development (§2.11) Short sprints deliver working software every few weeks. Requirements live in a backlog that changes every sprint. Agile is the most widely used evolutionary approach in industry today (Scrum, Kanban, etc.). Evolutionary development vs Iterative Enhancement (§2.7) Exams often contrast these two — read §2.7 in full, but summarise here: AspectEvolutionary developmentIterative Enhancement Overall architectureIn evolutionary development, the architecture may emerge or change during early versions.In iterative enhancement, the architecture is usually defined upfront and kept stable. RequirementsEvolutionary requirements are highly fluid and co-evolve with the product.Iterative enhancement keeps requirements more stable while each iteration adds planned modules. Goal of iterationEach evolutionary iteration aims to learn from users and adapt the product.Each iterative-enhancement iteration aims to implement the next planned part of a known structure. Example metaphorEvolutionary development is like growing an organism over time.Iterative enhancement is like filling in the rooms of a house that was already designed. Process view — how an evolutionary project unfolds Vision and priorities. The team defines a thin first release (a minimum viable product) that includes only must-have features. Build version 1. The team accepts some imperfection but documents known gaps and technical debt items openly. Deploy and gather feedback. Real users reveal missing business rules, performance problems, and new requirements. Reprioritise backlog. The product owner chooses the next features and fixes for version 2 based on feedback. Refactor and extend. The team improves architecture where needed, adds modules, and runs regression tests before the next release. Repeat the cycle until acceptance criteria for the full product are met or the product is retired. Advantages of evolutionary development Handles changing requirements — business, law, and competition change; evolutionary models absorb change without treating it as project failure. Early business value — partial system can generate revenue, support pilots, or satisfy regulators incrementally. Continuous stakeholder engagement — users stay involved; reduces gap between expectation and delivery. Lower risk of total wrong product — mistakes corrected in the next version rather than after a multi-year monolith fails acceptance. Morale and transparency — visible progress motivates team and sponsors. Disadvantages and risks Scope creep — without disciplined backlog control, “just one more feature” never ends; budgets explode. Architecture erosion — quick fixes in version 1 and 2 make version 3 slow and buggy unless refactoring is scheduled. Hard upfront estimation — total cost and end date are uncertain; fixed-price contracts are difficult. Documentation lag — teams may skip updating specs; maintenance and audit suffer. Process discipline required — evolution is not chaos; needs product owner, priorities, and quality gates. Integration complexity — each new version must integrate with existing modules and data; regression testing load grows. When to use evolutionary development Requirements are incomplete, volatile, or market-driven (e-commerce, social apps, internal tools). Competitive pr

title: 2.7 Iterative Enhancement Models
headings: Process — how an iterative enhancement project unfolds
Architecture and interface planning (critical success factor)
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.7 Iterative Enhancement Models 2.7 Iterative Enhancement Models ← Topic 2 Iterative Enhancement is an approach to software development in which the system is built through a series of iterations, and each iteration adds new modules or improves existing ones on top of a pre-planned overall architecture. Unlike pure evolutionary models (§2.6), where structure and requirements may co-evol
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.7 Iterative Enhancement Models 2.7 Iterative Enhancement Models ← Topic 2 Iterative Enhancement is an approach to software development in which the system is built through a series of iterations, and each iteration adds new modules or improves existing ones on top of a pre-planned overall architecture. Unlike pure evolutionary models (§2.6), where structure and requirements may co-evolve unpredictably, iterative enhancement assumes that the team can define a stable architectural skeleton early and then fill it in over successive cycles — like completing the rooms of a house whose floor plan was already drawn. Iterative Enhancement is a development philosophy, not always a single named diagram. The Incremental Process Model (§2.8) is the most common exam example of iterative enhancement in practice. Iterative Waterfall (§2.3) can also be used inside each iteration when the team needs controlled feedback within a single increment. The key exam phrase: “planned architecture + successive module additions.” Iterative Enhancement — each iteration adds modules to a pre-planned architecture (GeeksforGeeks). In exams, show a fixed core structure with new boxes attached in each cycle. Why iterative enhancement exists Classical Waterfall tries to build everything in one pass — risky when the system is large or when stakeholders need early proof that the direction is correct. Pure evolutionary development (Agile, evolutionary prototyping) allows architecture to emerge — powerful for uncertain domains but risky when the system must integrate with legacy databases, comply with standards, or scale to millions of users. Iterative Enhancement sits in the middle ground: plan the structure once, then grow the product in controlled steps so that each iteration delivers value without redesigning the foundation every month. Core principles Architecture first. Before the first iteration, architects define modules, interfaces, data models, and integration points. Later iterations must fit this skeleton unless a formal change request authorises structural revision. Each iteration extends the same codebase. New modules plug into existing interfaces; the team does not throw away the previous iteration and start from scratch (contrast throwaway prototyping, §2.4). Requirements are mostly known but may be refined. The broad feature set is planned early; details within each module can be clarified during the iteration that implements that module. Integration and regression testing every cycle. Because each iteration builds on prior work, the team must verify that new code does not break old modules — configuration management and automated tests are essential. Partial delivery is possible. When combined with the Incremental model, users may receive working subsets early; when used internally (e.g., alpha builds), stakeholders still see progress before the full product is finished. Process — how an iterative enhancement project unfolds Initial planning and architecture. The team produces a high-level requirements overview, system architecture document, module breakdown, and interface specifications. Risk analysis identifies which modules are critical path or technically risky. Prioritise modules for iteration 1. Choose the smallest set that forms a coherent, testable subset — often core data handling, authentication, or the most essential business workflow. Iteration 1 — develop, integrate, test, deliver. Run requirements → design → code → test for the chosen modules; integrate with any stubs for future modules; deliver to users or internal testers if the Incremental delivery pattern is used. Review and refine plan. Feedback from iteration 1 may adjust details of later modules but should not casually rewrite the whole architecture. Major structural changes go through change control. Iteration 2, 3, … n. Add the next planned modules, connect them via defined interfaces, run full regression on all prior modules, and optionally release another increment to customers. Final integration and acceptance. When all planned modules exist, perform system-wide testing, performance tuning, documentation, and formal acceptance against the original vision. What happens inside one iteration Each iteration is not “random coding” — it typically follows a mini-lifecycle (often a mini-Waterfall or Iterative Waterfall). Examiners expect you to name the activities: Requirements for this iteration — detailed specs for the modules in scope only. Design — module-level design that conforms to the global architecture (class diagrams, APIs, database tables). Implementation — coding with reuse of shared libraries and adherence to interface contracts. Unit and integration testing — test new modules alone and together with existing ones. Regression testing — re-run tests for all previously delivered modules to catch breakage. Release or internal demo — deploy increment or show working build to stakeholders. Iterative Enhancement vs Evolutionary Development This is the comparison most often asked in long questions. §2.6 covers evolutionary development in full; the table below is the exam-ready summary: AspectIterative EnhancementEvolutionary Development ArchitecturePlanned upfront and kept as a stable skeleton; major changes need formal approval.May emerge or change significantly across early versions; refactoring is expected. RequirementsMostly known at the start; refined per module during each iteration.Often unclear initially; discovered and changed through user experience of each version. Goal of each iterationImplement the next planned part of a known structure.Learn from users and adapt the product — structure and features may shift. Planning horizonDetailed plan for the whole system architecture; detailed requirements per iteration.Roadmap is high-level; detailed planning mainly for the next version or sprint. ExamplesIncremental model (§2.8); structured multi-release projects with fixed platforms.Evolutionary prototyping (§2.4), Spiral (§2.5), Agile (§2.11). Architecture riskLower — structure is fixed early, so integration paths are clearer.Higher — “big ball of mud” if refactoring is neglected between versions. Requirements riskHigher if upfront requirements were wrong — harder to pivot than in Agile.Lower — change is built into the process from the start. MetaphorFilling in rooms of a house already designed on paper.Growing an organism that changes shape as it matures. Iterative Enhancement vs Iterative Waterfall (§2.3) AspectIterative EnhancementIterative Waterfall Primary goalBuild the full system in planned stages, adding modules each iteration.Build one complete product through one pass of phases, looping back only to fix errors. Customer deliveryMay deliver partial working software after each iteration (especially with Incremental model).Customer usually receives the full product only at the end. Feedback loopsForward progress through planned increments; feedback refines future modules.Backward loops fix defects in earlier phases of the same release. ArchitectureExplicitly planned for all future iterations from the start.Designed once for the whole system; loops do not imply multiple customer releases. Iterative Enhancement vs Incremental Model (§2.8) Important exam distinction: Iterative Enhancement is the concept (planned architecture, successive module additions). The Incremental Process Model is a specific SDLC model that implements iterative enhancement by delivering each chunk through a mini-Waterfall to the customer. Not every iterative enhancement project must ship to customers every iteration — internal alpha builds still follow the philosophy. Every Incremental model project is iterative enhancement; not every iterative enhancement project uses the full Incremental delivery pattern. Architecture and interface planning (critical success factor) Because later iterations depend on decisions made early, the team must invest in: Modular decomposition — clear boundaries so iterati

When to use the Incremental Model
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.8 Incremental Process Model 2.8 Incremental Process Model ← Topic 2 The Incremental Process Model (also called the Incremental Model) divides the system into multiple increments — self-contained chunks of functionality — and develops each increment through a mini-Waterfall (requirements → design → implementation → testing) before delivering it to the customer. The first increment provi
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.8 Incremental Process Model 2.8 Incremental Process Model ← Topic 2 The Incremental Process Model (also called the Incremental Model) divides the system into multiple increments — self-contained chunks of functionality — and develops each increment through a mini-Waterfall (requirements → design → implementation → testing) before delivering it to the customer. The first increment provides core, usable capability; later increments add features until the full system is complete. Unlike Classical Waterfall, users do not wait until the end of the project to see working software. The Incremental model is the most common named SDLC model that implements Iterative Enhancement (§2.7): architecture is planned upfront, and each cycle adds modules on that stable base. It is also classified as an evolutionary approach (§2.6) because the product grows over time — but evolution here is in delivered features, not in discovering the whole architecture from scratch. The key exam phrase: “multiple mini-Waterfalls, each delivering a usable increment.” Incremental Model — each increment is a complete mini-cycle delivering usable functionality (GeeksforGeeks). In exams, draw stacked or side-by-side mini-Waterfalls labelled Increment 1, 2, 3… Why the Incremental model exists Large systems take months or years to build. Classical Waterfall keeps customers waiting until a single “big-bang” release — if requirements were misunderstood, the project fails late and expensively. Pure Agile suits many products but not every organisation (heavy regulation, fixed-price contracts, legacy integration). The Incremental model offers a structured middle path: plan the whole architecture once, ship working subsets on a schedule, gather feedback, and adjust later increments — without abandoning documentation or phase discipline entirely. Core characteristics Partitioned requirements. The full SRS is split into increment-sized pieces, usually ordered by business priority (must-have first, nice-to-have later). Mini-Waterfall per increment. Each chunk runs its own requirements refinement, design, coding, and testing before release — not a single monolithic pass through all phases for the whole system. Customer-visible delivery every increment. Each release is production-quality (or pilot-quality) software the user can operate, not merely an internal prototype. Shared architecture from day one. Database schema, module interfaces, and integration strategy must accommodate all planned increments — see §2.7 on architecture planning. Cumulative product. Increment 2 builds on increment 1’s codebase; increment 3 includes everything from 1 and 2 plus new features — the system grows, it is not replaced each time. Regression testing across increments. Every new increment must be tested together with all prior increments to ensure nothing breaks. Process — how an incremental project unfolds Overall requirements and architecture. Define the full vision, module breakdown, interfaces, and data model. Identify which features belong to which increment (release plan). Prioritise increments. Rank by business value, dependency order, and risk — core workflows and shared infrastructure typically go in increment 1. Increment 1 — mini-Waterfall. Detailed requirements for increment 1 only → design → implement → test → deploy to users. Document known stubs or placeholders for future increments if needed. Customer feedback and plan adjustment. Users report gaps, usability issues, or new priorities. Adjust scope of increment 2 within the fixed architecture; major structural changes need change control. Increment 2, 3, … n. Repeat mini-Waterfall for each chunk; integrate with all prior code; run full regression; release to customers. Final system acceptance. When all increments are delivered, perform end-to-end system test, performance validation, training, and formal sign-off on the complete product. Phases inside one increment (mini-Waterfall) Examiners often ask you to list what happens within a single increment. Typical phases: Requirements analysis (for this increment) — refine specs for features in scope; update traceability matrix. Design — module and interface design consistent with the global architecture; update DDS for new parts only. Implementation — code new features; reuse shared libraries from earlier increments. Testing — unit tests for new code; integration tests with prior increments; regression suite for the whole product so far. Delivery / deployment — release to production or pilot environment; user training for new features; release notes. If defects found during testing belong to earlier increments, the team may apply Iterative Waterfall (§2.3) feedback within that increment — fix design or code and re-test before shipping. Planning increments — horizontal vs vertical slicing How you split the system affects success. Two common strategies (know both for exams): StrategyMeaningExampleTrade-off Horizontal (layer) slicingEach increment completes one architectural layer for part of the system.Increment 1: database schema for all modules; increment 2: all UI shells; increment 3: all business logic.Early increments may not be usable by end users — less common for customer delivery. Vertical (feature) slicingEach increment delivers an end-to-end slice of functionality through all layers.Increment 1: login and user profile; increment 2: order placement; increment 3: payment and invoicing.Preferred for incremental delivery — each release is usable and demo-able. Exam tip: When asked for an example increment plan, use vertical slices — examiners expect customer-visible functionality in every increment. Classical Waterfall vs Incremental Model AspectClassical WaterfallIncremental Model Delivery patternSingle release at the end of the project after all phases complete once.Multiple partial releases; each increment is delivered when its mini-Waterfall finishes. RequirementsComplete SRS frozen before design begins.High-level SRS for whole system; detailed specs per increment just before that increment starts. Customer involvementMainly at start (requirements) and end (acceptance).Continuous — users operate each increment and feedback shapes later ones. Risk of wrong productHigh — mistakes discovered only at final acceptance.Lower — early increments validate direction before full investment. ArchitectureDesigned once for the entire system before coding.Designed once upfront to support all increments; must allow safe extension. TestingSystem testing mainly at the end.Testing after every increment plus cumulative regression. Schedule visibilityWorking software invisible until late in the project.Visible progress after each increment — good for sponsors and morale. Cost profileLower process overhead if one pass succeeds.Higher overhead (repeated releases, regression) but less catastrophic failure risk. Incremental Model vs Iterative Waterfall (§2.3) AspectIncremental ModelIterative Waterfall Number of customer releasesMultiple — one per increment.Usually one final product; loops fix errors within the same release. Direction of workForward through planned increments, each adding features.Mainly forward through phases, with backward loops to correct earlier-phase mistakes. Scope of each cycleA subset of total functionality delivered as working software.The full product scope, revisiting phases only when defects demand it. RelationshipIterative Waterfall can run inside each increment’s mini-Waterfall.Does not by itself deliver partial products to customers. Incremental Model vs Iterative Enhancement (§2.7) Iterative Enhancement is the broader philosophy (planned architecture + successive module additions). The Incremental Process Model is a specific way to implement it with formal mini-Waterfalls and customer delivery each cycle. In exam answers: Incremental ⊂ Iterative Enhancement in practice; every Incremental project is iterative enhancement, but you could do iterative enhancement with internal alph

RAD is not a single model but a philosophy that can use incremental
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.9 Rapid Application Development (RAD) 2.9 Rapid Application Development (RAD) ← Topic 2 Rapid Application Development (RAD) emphasises speed through time-boxed delivery, heavy user involvement, and extensive use of CASE tools (Computer-Aided Software Engineering) for code generation, prototyping, and automated testing. RAD is not a single model but a philosophy that can use incremental or prototype approaches. RAD model — time-boxed phases with active user participation and CASE tool support (GeeksforGeeks). Key features Time-boxing. Each iteration has a fixed duration, and the team adjusts scope to fit the time-box rather than extending the deadline indefinitely. User involvement. Users take part in workshops (such as Joint Application Design sessions) throughout development, not only at the start and end. Prototyping. The team builds rapid user-interface and workflow prototypes so that requirements can be confirmed with real feedback. CASE tools. Computer-Aided Software Engineering tools support automated code generation from models, reusable components, and fourth-generation database environments. Small teams. RAD works best when skilled developers work in small groups that stay close to users and decision-makers. Phases (typical RAD lifecycle) Requirements planning. The team runs a workshop to agree goals, scope, and constraints for the application. User design. Users and developers create prototypes together to validate screens and workflows. Construction. Developers implement the system quickly, often using CASE tools and generated code. Cutover. The team completes testing, data conversion, and go-live activities to put the system into production. Advantages and disadvantages Advantages. RAD can achieve very fast delivery, high user satisfaction, and fewer misunderstandings because users stay involved throughout. Disadvantages. RAD needs committed users, skilled staff, and strong CASE support. It is not ideal for very large teams, and scalability or performance can suffer if the architecture is rushed. Exam question — RAD Q: Explain Rapid Application Development. What are its key features, and what are its limitations? Model answer points: Define RAD as a fast development approach that combines time-boxing, continuous user involvement, prototyping, and CASE tools. Describe the four phases in order: requirements planning, user design, construction, and cutover. State advantages such as speed, user satisfaction, and fewer requirement errors. State limitations such as the need for active users, small skilled teams, limited suitability for very large systems, and dependence on tools. ← 2.8 Incremental Process Model 2.10 RAD vs Waterfall →

full_text: Topic 2 — 2.10 RAD vs Waterfall 2.10 RAD vs Waterfall ← Topic 2 RAD and the Classical Waterfall represent opposite ends of the SDLC spectrum: Waterfall prioritises planning and documentation; RAD prioritises speed and user feedback. ParameterRADWaterfall SpeedRAD is very fast because work is organised in time-boxed iterations with rapid delivery.Waterfall is slow because the customer receives the product only after the full cycle completes. User involvementUsers are involved continuously through workshops and prototyping sessions.Users are involved mainly during requirements definition and final acceptance. RequirementsRequirements are allowed to evolve during development as users learn more.Requirements are fixed upfront in the SRS before construction begins. DocumentationRAD keeps documentation minimal and prefers working software as the main deliverable.Waterfall produces extensive documentation at each phase. ToolsRAD relies heavily on CASE tools and code generation.Waterfall typically uses standard development tools. Team sizeRAD works best with small, skilled teams working closely with users.Waterfall can support teams of any size, including very large teams. FlexibilityRAD is highly flexible because scope is adjusted to fit each time-box.Waterfall has low flexibility because changes become costly after sign-off. Best forRAD is best for small or medium applications, tight deadlines, and highly engaged users.Waterfall is best for fixed requirements, regulatory environments, and large documented projects. Exam question — RAD vs Waterfall Q: Compare RAD with the Traditional Waterfall SDLC. When would you choose each? Model answer points: Present a comparison using criteria such as speed, user involvement, requirements handling, documentation, tools, team size, and flexibility. Explain when RAD is appropriate, for example when the schedule is tight, users are available, scope is moderate, and CASE tools exist. Explain when Waterfall is appropriate, for example when requirements are stable, an audit trail is required, or the project is large and regulated. ← 2.9 Rapid Application Development (RAD) 2.11 Agile Software Development →

Agile — iterative sprints with continuous custom
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.11 Agile Software Development 2.11 Agile Software Development ← Topic 2 Agile is a family of lightweight, iterative SDLC approaches defined by the Agile Manifesto (2001). Agile values individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change over processes, documentation, contracts, and rigid plans. Agile — iterative sprints with continuous customer feedback (GeeksforGeeks). Agile Manifesto values Individuals and interactions over processes and tools. Working software over comprehensive documentation. Customer collaboration over contract negotiation. Responding to change over following a plan. Agile principles (selected for exams) Deliver working software frequently (weeks, not months). Welcome changing requirements, even late in development. Business people and developers cooperate daily. Teams should be self-organising and should reflect regularly so they can improve their process. Scrum sprints (common Agile framework) Sprint. A sprint is a fixed time-box (usually one to four weeks) in which the team delivers a potentially shippable product increment. Sprint planning. At the start of the sprint, the team selects which backlog items it will complete. Daily stand-up. Each day the team holds a short meeting to share progress and surface blockers. Sprint review. At the end of the sprint, the team demonstrates work to stakeholders and collects feedback. Retrospective. The team reviews what went well and what to improve before the next sprint begins. Advantages and disadvantages Advantages. Agile provides fast feedback, adapts well to change, keeps customers involved, and delivers business value early. Disadvantages. Schedule and cost are harder to predict, the customer must stay engaged, documentation may be lighter, and very large distributed teams need strong discipline to succeed. Exam question — Agile Q: Explain Agile software development. State the four values of the Agile Manifesto and describe how sprints work in Scrum. Model answer points: Define Agile as iterative, incremental, customer-collaborative development based on the Agile Manifesto (2001). State the four values: individuals and interactions, working software, customer collaboration, and responding to change are preferred over rigid process, heavy documentation, contracts, and fixed plans. Explain a Scrum sprint as a time-boxed iteration that includes planning, daily stand-ups, a review demo, and a retrospective. Mention advantages (flexibility and early delivery) and disadvantages (estimation difficulty and need for committed stakeholders). ← 2.10 RAD vs Waterfall 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) →

title: 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP)
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) ← Topic 2 Extreme Programming (XP), introduced by Kent Beck, is an Agile methodology that takes proven practices to extreme levels. XP focuses on technical excellence, frequent releases, and close customer pairing with developers. XP — engineering practices taken to the extreme within Agile (GeeksforGeeks). Core XP practices Pai
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) ← Topic 2 Extreme Programming (XP), introduced by Kent Beck, is an Agile methodology that takes proven practices to extreme levels. XP focuses on technical excellence, frequent releases, and close customer pairing with developers. XP — engineering practices taken to the extreme within Agile (GeeksforGeeks). Core XP practices Pair programming. Two developers share one workstation: the driver writes code while the navigator reviews and thinks ahead, and they swap roles frequently. Test-Driven Development (TDD). The developer writes a failing test first, writes the minimal code to pass it, and then refactors (the Red, Green, Refactor cycle). Continuous Integration (CI). The team merges code into a shared repository several times a day and runs automated build and test on each merge. Collective code ownership. Any member of the team may improve any part of the codebase. Simple design. The team builds only what is needed now, following the “You Aren’t Gonna Need It” (YAGNI) principle. Refactoring. The team continuously improves code structure without changing external behaviour. On-site customer. A user representative is available full-time to answer questions and clarify requirements. Small releases. The team delivers small, deployable increments frequently rather than waiting for one large release. Advantages and disadvantages Advantages. XP tends to produce high code quality, fewer defects through TDD and pair review, rapid feedback, and strong communication within the team. Disadvantages. Pair programming increases staffing cost on coding tasks, the approach needs highly skilled developers, an on-site customer is often impractical, and XP is less suited to very large teams or fixed-price contracts. Exam question — Extreme Programming Q: Describe Extreme Programming. Explain pair programming, TDD, and Continuous Integration with advantages and disadvantages. Model answer points: Define XP as an Agile method introduced by Kent Beck that takes good practices to an extreme level for quality and speed. Explain pair programming as two developers sharing one keyboard for real-time review and knowledge sharing. Explain TDD as writing a test first, then code, then refactoring, which improves coverage and keeps design simple. Explain continuous integration as frequent merges with automated tests that catch defects early. State advantages (quality and fewer defects) and disadvantages (cost of pairing, need for skilled staff and an available customer). MCQ (GFG style): In TDD, the first step is — (A) Write a failing test. ← 2.11 Agile Software Development 2.13 SDLC V-Model →

The left side of the "V" represents development activities; the right side represents testing activities that verify those development stages. V-Model — each development phase has a matching test phase (Geeksfo
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.13 SDLC V-Model 2.13 SDLC V-Model ← Topic 2 The V-Model (Verification and Validation model) extends Waterfall by mapping each development phase to a corresponding testing phase. The left side of the "V" represents development activities; the right side represents testing activities that verify those development stages. V-Model — each development phase has a matching test phase (GeeksforGeeks). Verification vs Validation Verification asks whether we are building the product right. It checks conformance to specifications through reviews, inspections, and unit or integration testing. Validation asks whether we are building the right product. It checks that user needs are met through acceptance testing and user acceptance testing (UAT). Phase pairs (left → right) Development (verification)Testing (validation of that stage) Requirements analysisRequirements analysis on the left side of the V is verified by acceptance testing on the right. System designSystem design is paired with system testing to confirm that the overall system meets its specification. Architecture / high-level designArchitecture or high-level design is paired with integration testing to verify that subsystems work together. Module designDetailed module design is paired with unit testing to verify each component in isolation. Advantages and disadvantages Advantages. Testing is planned from the start, traceability links each requirement to tests, and the model suits safety-critical and regulated systems. Disadvantages. The V-Model shares Waterfall’s rigidity, provides no early working software, makes late requirement changes expensive, and requires maintaining testing documents alongside design documents. Exam question — V-Model Q: Explain the V-Model. Differentiate verification and validation. Map development phases to test phases. Model answer points: Describe the V-Model as Waterfall with a parallel testing arm: the left side is development and the right side is testing. Define verification as building the product correctly against specifications, and validation as building the product the user actually needs. Map phase pairs: requirements with acceptance testing, system design with system testing, high-level design with integration testing, and module design with unit testing. State advantages (early test planning and traceability) and disadvantages (inflexibility, late delivery, and costly changes). ← 2.12 Extreme Programming (XP) 2.14 Information System Life Cycle →

title: 2.14 Information System Life Cycle
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.14 Information System Life Cycle 2.14 Information System Life Cycle ← Topic 2 The Information System (IS) Life Cycle describes phases specific to developing information systems that collect, process, store, and distribute information for organisational decision-making. It extends generic SDLC with IS-specific concerns such as data migration, user training, and organisational change man
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.14 Information System Life Cycle 2.14 Information System Life Cycle ← Topic 2 The Information System (IS) Life Cycle describes phases specific to developing information systems that collect, process, store, and distribute information for organisational decision-making. It extends generic SDLC with IS-specific concerns such as data migration, user training, and organisational change management. Information System Life Cycle — phases from initiation to retirement (GeeksforGeeks). IS-specific phases Initiation / feasibility. The organisation identifies the business need, defines scope, performs cost–benefit analysis, and obtains stakeholder approval. Requirements determination. Analysts study the information needs of users and departments that the system will support. Analysis. The team models current and proposed processes using tools such as data-flow and entity–relationship diagrams and performs gap analysis. Design. Architects define system architecture, database design, user interface, security, and integration interfaces. Implementation. Developers code the system, convert data, test the solution, and train users. Operation and maintenance. The organisation monitors performance, handles change requests, and maintains backups while the system is live. Retirement / disposal. When the system is replaced, data is migrated and the legacy system is decommissioned safely. IS-specific concerns Organisational impact. A new information system changes workflows, so change management and communication are critical. Data quality. Poor source data leads to poor decisions, so data cleansing during migration is essential. Security and compliance. The system must provide access control, audit trails, and compliance with relevant regulations. User adoption. Non-technical staff need training, help-desk support, and clear documentation to use the system effectively. Exam question — IS Life Cycle Q: Describe the Information System Life Cycle. How does it differ from generic SDLC? Model answer points: Define the Information System Life Cycle as the set of phases used to build systems that manage organisational information and support decisions. List the phases in order from initiation through requirements, analysis, design, implementation, operation, and retirement. Explain IS-specific concerns such as organisational change, data migration, user training, compliance, and data quality. Contrast with generic SDLC by noting that IS life cycles explicitly include business processes and people, not only software construction. ← 2.13 SDLC V-Model 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle →

title: 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle ← Topic 2 The Database Application System Life Cycle focuses on systems where a database is the central component. Phases emphasise data modelling, schema design, normalisation, query optimisation, and data integrity alongside application logic. Database Application Life Cycle — data-centred phases wi
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle ← Topic 2 The Database Application System Life Cycle focuses on systems where a database is the central component. Phases emphasise data modelling, schema design, normalisation, query optimisation, and data integrity alongside application logic. Database Application Life Cycle — data-centred phases within SDLC (GeeksforGeeks). DB application phases Planning. The team defines scope, identifies data sources and users, and confirms feasibility. Requirements collection and analysis. Analysts document data needs, transactions, reports, and constraints. Conceptual data modelling. A high-level entity–relationship diagram defines entities, relationships, and attributes. Logical database design. The schema is normalised (for example to third normal form), and integrity rules are defined. Physical database design. The team chooses storage structures, indexes, partitioning, and performance tuning options. Application development. Developers implement SQL, stored procedures, the application layer, and APIs. Testing. Testers verify data integrity, run load tests, and confirm backup and recovery procedures. Deployment and migration. Legacy data is loaded, the system is cut over, and parallel running may be used to reduce risk. Maintenance. The team manages schema evolution, performance monitoring, and security patches over the life of the database. DB-specific concerns Data integrity. Primary keys, foreign keys, constraints, and triggers keep data consistent and valid. Concurrency. Locking and transaction isolation allow many users to access the database safely at the same time. Backup and recovery. Disaster recovery plans and replication protect against data loss. Scalability. Indexing strategies and sharding help the database grow as data volume increases. Exam question — DB Application Life Cycle Q: Explain the Database Application System Life Cycle. What phases are specific to database development? Model answer points: Define the Database Application System Life Cycle as an SDLC variant in which the database is the central architectural component. Highlight database-specific phases: conceptual modelling with an ERD, logical design with normalisation, and physical design with indexes and storage tuning. Also mention data migration, integrity testing, backup and recovery, and concurrency control. Explain how this differs from generic SDLC through deeper data modelling and long-term schema maintenance. ← 2.14 Information System Life Cycle 2.16 Comparison of Life Cycle Models →

full_text: Topic 2 — 2.16 Comparison of Life Cycle Models 2.16 Comparison of Life Cycle Models ← Topic 2 No single SDLC model fits all projects. Examiners often ask for a master comparison and selection criteria. Use the table below as a revision anchor. SDLC models overview — from sequential (Waterfall) to highly iterative (Agile) (GeeksforGeeks). Master comparison table ModelNatureRequirementsRisk handlingDeliveryBest suited for WaterfallIt follows a strictly sequential process.Requirements are fixed upfront.Risks are often discovered late.The customer receives the product at the end of the project.It suits stable specifications and small or simple projects. Iterative WaterfallIt is sequential but allows controlled feedback between phases.Requirements are mostly fixed, with limited revision.Some risk is reduced through backtracking when errors are found.Delivery still occurs mainly at the end of the project.It suits projects with moderate requirement stability. PrototypeIt is iterative and centred on building prototypes.Requirements are unclear at first and are clarified through prototyping.Early user feedback reduces requirements risk.A prototype is delivered first, then the final product.It suits novel user interfaces and vague initial requirements. SpiralIt is iterative and explicitly risk-driven.Requirements evolve across cycles.Risks are analysed and addressed in every cycle.Deliverables are produced at the end of each spiral cycle.It suits large, complex, high-risk projects. IncrementalIt uses multiple mini-Waterfall cycles.Requirements are partitioned into increments.Partial early delivery reduces overall delivery risk.Each increment is delivered separately to the customer.It suits projects with prioritised features that can be released in parts. RADIt is time-boxed and tool-driven.Requirements evolve during rapid development.User workshops help surface risks early.Working software is delivered in fast iterations.It suits small or medium projects with tight schedules. Agile/XPIt uses short iterative sprints.Requirements are highly changeable.Continuous customer feedback manages risk.Working software is delivered every sprint.It suits dynamic requirements and fast-changing markets. V-ModelIt is sequential with paired test phases.Requirements are fixed upfront.Test planning begins early, which improves risk control.Delivery occurs at the end of the project.It suits safety-critical and regulated systems. Selection criteria (exam checklist) Project size. Small projects often suit RAD or Agile, whereas large projects may need the Spiral model, Incremental delivery, or a structured Waterfall or V-Model approach. Requirements stability. Stable requirements favour Waterfall or the V-Model, while volatile requirements favour Agile or the Prototype model. Risk level. High-risk projects benefit from the Spiral model, whereas lower-risk projects can use Waterfall or Incremental approaches. Customer availability. When customers can participate frequently, Agile or RAD is appropriate; when they cannot, Waterfall with a detailed SRS is more practical. Time-to-market. Urgent delivery favours RAD, Agile, or Incremental models, while a flexible schedule allows Waterfall or the V-Model. Regulatory and documentation needs. Strict audit or safety requirements favour the V-Model or Waterfall, whereas minimal documentation pressure allows a lighter Agile process. Exam question — Model comparison and selection Q: Compare major SDLC models in a table. What criteria should a project manager use to select a model? Model answer points: Draw the master comparison table and explain each model’s nature, requirements handling, risk handling, delivery pattern, and best use in full sentences. Explain selection criteria such as project size, requirements stability, risk level, customer involvement, schedule pressure, and documentation needs. Support the answer with scenario examples, such as a hospital system (V-Model), a startup app (Agile), or an ERP module delivered in increments. Conclude that no single model is best for every project because the right choice depends on context. ← 2.15 Database Application System Life Cycle 2.17 Pairwise Model Comparisons →

full_text: Topic 2 — 2.17 Pairwise Model Comparisons 2.17 Pairwise Model Comparisons ← Topic 2 Exams frequently ask "difference between X and Y" questions. Below are the four most common pairwise comparisons with tabular answers. Waterfall vs Incremental AspectWaterfallIncremental DeliveryWaterfall delivers the complete product in a single release at the end.The Incremental model delivers multiple partial releases throughout the project. FeedbackCustomer feedback arrives late, after the full system is built.Customer feedback arrives early, after each increment is delivered. RequirementsWaterfall expects requirements to be complete before development starts.The Incremental model partitions requirements by priority across increments. RiskWaterfall carries a high risk of building the wrong product if direction was wrong from the start.The Incremental model lowers that risk because early increments validate the project direction. ArchitectureWaterfall designs the architecture once for the whole system.The Incremental model must plan for extensibility upfront so later increments can be added safely. Waterfall vs Spiral AspectWaterfallSpiral IterationsWaterfall usually runs as a single pass, or allows feedback loops only in its iterative variant.The Spiral model runs multiple risk-driven cycles until the product is complete. Risk analysisWaterfall treats risk analysis as implicit and often discovers problems late.The Spiral model makes risk analysis explicit in every cycle. CostWaterfall is lower in cost for simple projects with stable requirements.The Spiral model is higher in cost because it uses multiple cycles and prototypes. PrototypingPrototyping is not built into classical Waterfall.The Spiral model uses prototypes whenever they help resolve risks. Best forWaterfall is best for small projects with stable requirements.The Spiral model is best for large, complex, high-risk projects. Agile vs V-Model AspectAgileV-Model FlexibilityAgile is very flexible and welcomes changing requirements throughout the project.The V-Model has low flexibility because requirements and test plans are fixed early. DocumentationAgile keeps documentation minimal and prioritises working software.The V-Model requires extensive test and design documentation for each phase. TestingTesting runs continuously throughout every sprint.Testing is planned in pairs that align with each development phase. Customer roleThe customer collaborates with the team daily or weekly.The customer is involved mainly during requirements definition and final acceptance. Best forAgile is best for dynamic requirements and startup-style products.The V-Model is best for regulated and safety-critical systems. V-Model vs Waterfall AspectV-ModelWaterfall TestingThe V-Model plans testing from the start and pairs each development phase with a matching test phase.Classical Waterfall concentrates testing mainly after the coding phase is complete. TraceabilityThe V-Model provides strong traceability from each requirement to its corresponding tests.Waterfall provides weaker traceability unless the team maintains it manually. StructureThe V-Model uses the same sequential development phases as Waterfall but adds a parallel testing arm.Waterfall follows linear phases without formally paired test stages. Verification/ValidationThe V-Model makes an explicit distinction between verification and validation.Waterfall does not formally distinguish verification from validation. Use caseThe V-Model is used for medical devices, aerospace, and banking systems that need strict compliance.Waterfall is used for general projects with fixed requirements and conventional documentation needs. Exam question — Pairwise comparisons Q: Differentiate: (a) Waterfall vs Incremental, (b) Waterfall vs Spiral, (c) Agile vs V-Model, (d) V-Model vs Waterfall. Model answer points: Use the four comparison tables above and write at least four or five complete comparison points for each pair. Explain key contrasts such as delivery timing, risk, flexibility, testing approach, and the customer’s role in the project. Always state when each model is preferred, not only how the two models differ. MCQ (GFG style): Which model is most suitable for high-risk, large projects? — (C) Spiral Model. ← 2.16 Comparison of Life Cycle Models 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC →

title: 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC
headings: Risk management process (three sets — GFG series)
excerpt: Topic 2 — 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC ← Topic 2 Risk management is the systematic process of identifying, analysing, prioritising, and mitigating risks that could threaten project success. While any SDLC model benefits from risk management, the Spiral model (§2.5) embeds it explicitly in every cycle. Risk management connects to Topic 1's theme of reducing project fail
full_text: Topic 2 — 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC ← Topic 2 Risk management is the systematic process of identifying, analysing, prioritising, and mitigating risks that could threaten project success. While any SDLC model benefits from risk management, the Spiral model (§2.5) embeds it explicitly in every cycle. Risk management connects to Topic 1's theme of reducing project failure (§1.13). Risk management integrated into SDLC — strongest in the Spiral model (GeeksforGeeks). Risk management process (three sets — GFG series) Set 1 — Identification and classification Identify risks. The team brainstorms technical, schedule, cost, staffing, and external risks that could affect the project. Classify risks. Each risk is grouped as a project risk (budget or schedule), a technical risk (technology or performance), or a business risk (market or compliance). Risk items. Every risk is recorded with a clear description, its source, and the SDLC phase it would affect. Set 2 — Analysis and prioritisation Probability. The team estimates how likely each risk is to occur (high, medium, or low). Impact. The team estimates the consequence if the risk happens, such as cost overrun, product failure, or schedule delay. Risk exposure. Exposure is calculated as probability multiplied by impact, and risks are ranked by this score. Risk matrix. Risks are plotted on a probability–impact grid so the team can see which ones need attention first. Set 3 — Mitigation, monitoring, and contingency Mitigation strategies. The team may avoid the risk by changing the plan, transfer it (for example through insurance or outsourcing), mitigate it by reducing probability or impact, or accept it and monitor it only. Contingency plans. For important risks, the team prepares predefined actions such as switching to a backup vendor or reducing scope if the risk occurs. Monitoring. Risk indicators are tracked throughout the SDLC and reassessed at major milestones. Link to Spiral. In the Spiral model, quadrant 2 (identify and resolve risks) carries out this process in every cycle. Common software project risks Requirements may change or remain incomplete throughout the project. The chosen technology may become obsolete or may be unproven at the required scale. Key personnel may leave, taking critical knowledge with them. The team may underestimate system complexity or integration difficulty. Security vulnerabilities may be discovered only late in testing or after release. Exam question — Risk Management in SDLC Q: Explain how risk management is integrated into the SDLC. Describe the risk management process and link it to the Spiral model. Model answer points: Explain that risk management means identifying, analysing, prioritising, mitigating, and monitoring risks throughout the SDLC. Describe Set 1: identify and classify risks as project, technical, or business risks. Describe Set 2: estimate probability and impact, compute exposure, and use a risk matrix to rank risks. Describe Set 3: choose strategies (avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept), prepare contingency plans, and monitor risks continuously. Link to the Spiral model: risk analysis in quadrant 2 of every cycle is the strongest built-in integration of risk management in an SDLC model. Cross-reference §2.5 and Topic 1 §1.13 where relevant. ← 2.17 Pairwise Model Comparisons Key takeaways →

An SDLC model (Waterfall, Spiral, Agile, and others) defines how those phases are ordered or repeated. Classical Waterfall is strictly sequential and suits stable requirements, but it delivers working software late and resists change. Iterative Waterfall keeps the same phase ord
full_text: Topic 2 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 2 SDLC is a phased framework from planning to maintenance. An SDLC model (Waterfall, Spiral, Agile, and others) defines how those phases are ordered or repeated. Classical Waterfall is strictly sequential and suits stable requirements, but it delivers working software late and resists change. Iterative Waterfall keeps the same phase order but allows controlled feedback when later phases discover errors in earlier work. Prototype model / Software Prototyping uses early working models; throwaway prototyping clarifies requirements, while evolutionary prototyping grows the prototype into the final product. Spiral model (Boehm, 1986) is risk-driven, uses four quadrants per cycle, and fits large, high-risk projects. Evolutionary development builds successive versions so requirements and design co-evolve; examples include evolutionary prototyping, Spiral, Incremental, and Agile approaches. Iterative Enhancement plans architecture upfront and adds or refines modules in each iteration on that stable base. Incremental model delivers the system in chunks, each developed through a mini-Waterfall, so users receive partial working software early. RAD emphasises time-boxing, user workshops, and CASE tools for fast delivery when users are highly engaged. Agile follows the Manifesto values and short sprints. XP extends Agile with pair programming, test-driven development, and continuous integration. V-Model pairs each development phase with a test phase and distinguishes verification from validation; it suits regulated and safety-critical systems. IS and DB life cycles are domain-specific extensions of the generic SDLC for information systems and database applications. Model selection depends on project size, requirements stability, risk, customer availability, schedule pressure, and documentation needs. Risk management runs through identify, analyse, prioritise, mitigate, and monitor steps; the Spiral model embeds this explicitly in every cycle. ← 2.18 Risk Management in SDLC Topic 3 — Software Project Management →
